bibliographic_data.title: Secure Multi-Party Computation in Large Networks
bibliographic_data.abstract: We describe scalable protocols for solving the secure multi-party computation (MPC) problem among a large number of parties. We consider both the synchronous and the asynchronous communication models. In the synchronous setting, our protocol is secure against a static malicious adversary corrupting less than a 1/3 fraction of the parties. In the asynchronous setting, we allow the adversary to corrupt less than a 1/8 fraction of parties. For any deterministic function that can be computed by an arithmetic circuit with m gates, both of our protocols require each party to send a number of field elements and perform an amount of computation that is ˜O(m/n + √n). We also show that our protocols provide perfect and universally-composable security.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper presents scalable protocols for secure multi-party computation (MPC) in both synchronous and asynchronous communication models, providing perfect and universally-composable security.
core_content.key_methodology: The protocols rely on the notion of quorums to achieve sublinear communication and computation costs. The authors introduce a threshold counting problem and propose an efficient protocol to solve it in the asynchronous setting.
outcomes: Secure MPC protocols for both synchronous and asynchronous settings
Sublinear communication and computation costs per party
core_content.full_text_markdown: In this paper, we address the secure multi-party computation (MPC) problem in large networks. The authors propose scalable protocols that can handle a large number of parties while ensuring perfect and universally-composable security. For synchronous settings, the protocol is secure against up to 1/3 fraction of corrupted parties, whereas for asynchronous settings, it can tolerate up to 1/8 fraction of corrupted parties. These protocols are designed to compute any deterministic function represented by an arithmetic circuit with m gates, requiring each party to send a number of field elements and perform computation that is approximately O(m/n + √n). The key innovation lies in the use of quorums and solving the threshold counting problem to achieve these goals.
